title: Logic Apps Standard vs Consumption — Hosting, Deployment, Networking, Pricing

confirmed_facts.fact: Consumption: multi-tenant, one workflow per resource, pay-per-execution (action + trigger + connector), auto-scales. Resource type: Microsoft.Logic/workflows
Standard: single-tenant, multiple workflows per resource, runs on Azure App Service plan or ASE (WS1/WS2/WS3 plans). Resource type: Microsoft.Web/sites (kind: workflowapp)
Standard supports VNET integration (outbound) and private endpoints (inbound) — Consumption does NOT support VNET integration natively (requires ISE, which is deprecated).
Standard supports both stateful workflows (run history persisted, replay-able) and stateless workflows (in-memory only, much faster, no run history).
Standard workflow definitions are stored in files (workflow.json) — supports local development, version control, and CI/CD natively. Consumption stores definitions in the ARM resource.
Standard supports deployment slots (staging → production swap) — Consumption does not.
Standard built-in connectors run in-process (same compute as the workflow) — much faster and no connector fees. Managed connectors still run out-of-process.
Standard can be developed locally using VS Code + Azure Logic Apps (Standard) extension. Run/debug workflows locally before deploying.
Consumption limits: 250 actions per workflow run, 100K actions per 5 min per subscription per region. Standard limits: 500 actions per workflow run (stateful), higher throughput.
Standard uses connections.json and parameters.json files alongside workflow.json — separate from ARM template parameters. Connection strings use app settings references.
ISE (Integration Service Environment) is deprecated — Microsoft recommends Standard for VNET-isolated workloads. ISE will be retired in August 2024.
Standard pricing: base cost of the WS plan (WS1 ~$175/mo) + storage costs. No per-action fees for built-in connectors. Managed connector fees apply.

implementation_notes:
=== WHEN TO USE CONSUMPTION ===
- Simple, low-volume workflows (< 100 runs/day)
- Quick prototyping with portal designer
- Cost-sensitive workloads (pay-per-execution)
- No VNET requirement
- Single workflow per resource is acceptable

=== WHEN TO USE STANDARD ===
- High-volume or latency-sensitive workloads
- Need VNET integration or private endpoints
- Multiple related workflows in one resource
- CI/CD with deployment slots
- Local development and testing required
- Need stateless workflows for performance
- ISE migration target

=== STANDARD PROJECT STRUCTURE ===
```
logic-app-name/
  host.json                    # runtime config
  connections.json             # connection references
  parameters.json              # workflow parameters
  workflow1/
    workflow.json              # workflow definition
  workflow2/
    workflow.json
  .funcignore
  .vscode/
    settings.json              # local dev settings
```

=== DEPLOYMENT DIFFERENCES ===
Consumption: deploy via ARM template (PUT Microsoft.Logic/workflows)
Standard: deploy via ZIP deploy to the App Service (az webapp deploy) or GitHub Actions

Consumption ARM template:
```json
{"type": "Microsoft.Logic/workflows", "apiVersion": "2019-05-01", ...}
```

Standard: uses Microsoft.Web/sites with kind='workflowapp':
```json
{"type": "Microsoft.Web/sites", "kind": "workflowapp,functionapp", "apiVersion": "2023-01-01", ...}
```

=== MANAGED IDENTITY DIFFERENCES ===
Consumption: system-assigned MSI on the Logic App resource directly
Standard: system-assigned MSI on the App Service (same as Azure Functions)
Both use the same pattern for HTTP actions with MSI authentication.
caveats: Standard workflows use a DIFFERENT designer in the portal (and VS Code) — not all Consumption connectors are available in Standard yet
Standard managed connectors have different connection configuration (uses app settings, not $connections parameter)
Some Consumption-only connectors (e.g., SAP, IBM MQ) are not yet available as Standard built-in connectors
Stateless workflows do NOT persist run history — if you need debugging or audit trails, use stateful
Standard requires an App Service Plan (minimum WS1) — there is a monthly base cost even with zero executions
Migrating from Consumption to Standard is NOT automatic — workflow definitions are similar but connection wiring and deployment models differ
